content.text: Retention policy answers one question: how long should each category of log be kept before it is permanently deleted? For LLM applications, the answer is shorter than most teams expect. Logs that contain conversation content — even after PII scrubbing — still carry re-identification risk, accumulate storage costs proportional to traffic, and create legal liability in the event of a breach. The principle is to compute aggregates quickly and delete the raw records, not to retain raw logs indefinitely "in case they are useful later."

attrs.alt: LLM log lifecycle: hot storage (0-30 days) → warm (31-90 days) → cold archive (91 days - 12 months) → permanent deletion. Policy table showing retention windows by log type.

content.text: What It Is
Log retention policy

is the set of rules that determines how long each category of log record is kept before it is permanently and irrecoverably deleted. For LLM applications, retention policy is not an optional operational concern — it is a privacy obligation, a cost control lever, and a security risk management tool simultaneously. The wrong retention window (too long) creates regulatory liability and unnecessary re-identification risk; too short creates gaps in your ability to investigate incidents or comply with audit requirements.
Retention policy operates on
log categories

, not on individual records. You define a policy per category (operational metrics, security audit logs, conversation content logs, aggregate summaries) and the storage layer enforces the window automatically via lifecycle rules, partition drops, or TTL indexes. The goal is to make expiry structural — built into the schema at creation time — rather than reactive, where you attempt to delete logs after the fact in response to a user complaint or regulator inquiry.
Why It Matters

The standard instinct of engineering teams is to keep logs forever "in case they are useful later." For LLM applications, this instinct is actively harmful. Conversation logs — even after scrubbing obvious PII — retain
re-identification risk

: an adversary with access to other datasets can sometimes reconstruct a user's identity from the behavioral fingerprint embedded in their conversation patterns. The 2006 Netflix prize dataset de-anonymization, where researchers re-identified users from "anonymized" movie ratings alone, established that partial data is not safe data.
Concrete pain points when retention is unmanaged:
GDPR Article 5(1)(e) — storage limitation:

Personal data must not be kept longer than necessary for the purpose it was collected. Retaining conversation logs for 5 years when you only need 30 days for debugging is a direct violation. Fines reach €20M or 4% of global annual turnover.
Breach blast radius:
A log store breach exposes every record retained. Logs from 3 years ago are just as damaging as logs from yesterday. Shorter retention windows minimize the damage of any future breach.
Storage cost:

At 1,000 requests/day with 2 KB per log record, a year of raw conversation logs is ~730 MB — manageable. At 100,000 requests/day, that becomes 73 GB/year, growing linearly. S3 + Glacier costs $0.004/GB/month; for large LLM deployments, unjustified retention adds thousands per year.
Right to erasure requests:
When a user invokes GDPR Article 17, you must delete their data within 30 days. If you don't know where all their logs are (because retention is unmanaged), compliance is impossible.
Benefits of a Structured Retention Policy
Reduced liability exposure:
Data you don't have cannot be subpoenaed, breached, or misused. Deleting on schedule is affirmative legal protection.
Predictable storage costs:
With lifecycle policies in place, storage costs scale with request volume but are bounded by the retention window, not by the total historical volume.
Faster erasure compliance:
When logs are categorized and stored in partitioned tables or prefixed S3 paths per log category, deleting a user's records is a targeted operation rather than a full-table scan.
Preserving long-term analytics without PII:
By aggregating raw logs into PII-free metrics before deletion, you retain indefinite historical visibility into model performance, cost, and quality without retaining any personal data.
Cleaner incident investigation:
When retention windows are well-defined, engineers know exactly how far back operational logs reach for debugging, reducing time spent querying empty date ranges.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Trade-off
Description
Mitigation
Loss of debuggability
Short windows mean you can't investigate incidents reported 45 days after occurrence
Retain aggregate error metrics indefinitely; invest in real-time alerting
Implementation complexity
Partitioned tables, lifecycle rules, aggregation jobs all require upfront engineering
Build once during initial logging setup; retrofit is much harder
Legal hold conflicts
Ongoing litigation may require preserving logs beyond your normal retention window
Build a legal hold flag that suspends lifecycle rules for specific records
Aggregation job failures
If aggregate-before-delete job fails, deleting raw logs loses data permanently
Run aggregation job on N-day lag; only delete after verifying aggregate row exists
When to Apply — Choosing Retention Windows
Retention windows are determined by three overlapping constraints:
operational need
(how long you actually use the logs for debugging),
regulatory minimum
(what the law requires you to keep, e.g., 12-month security audit logs for SOC 2), and
privacy maximum

(the shortest window that satisfies operational and regulatory needs, because GDPR requires minimization). The retention window is the intersection: at least the regulatory minimum, at most the privacy maximum, long enough for operational use.

from enum import Enum
from dataclasses import dataclass


class LogCategory(Enum):
    OPERATIONAL = "operational"   # latency, tokens, cost — no content
    CONTENT = "content"           # prompt/response snippets (even redacted)
    SECURITY = "security"         # auth, access, key usage
    AGGREGATE = "aggregate"       # daily summaries, no PII
    DELETION_AUDIT = "deletion"   # proof of erasure (contains no PII itself)


@dataclass
class RetentionPolicy:
    category: LogCategory
    hot_days: int          # fast storage (ElasticSearch, CloudWatch)
    warm_days: int         # compressed queryable (S3 + Athena, Parquet)
    archive_days: int | None  # cold archive (Glacier); None = skip
    total_days: int | None    # None = keep indefinitely (aggregates only)
    justification: str


POLICIES = [
    RetentionPolicy(
        LogCategory.OPERATIONAL, 30, 60, None, 90,
        "90-day debugging window; aggregates survive indefinitely"
    ),
    RetentionPolicy(
        LogCategory.CONTENT, 14, 14, None, 30,
        "GDPR minimization: even redacted content carries re-ID risk"
    ),
    RetentionPolicy(
        LogCategory.SECURITY, 90, 270, 365, 730,
        "SOC 2 requires 12 months; ISO 27001 recommends 24"
    ),
    RetentionPolicy(
        LogCategory.AGGREGATE, 0, 0, 0, None,
        "No PII — retain indefinitely for trend analysis"
    ),
    RetentionPolicy(
        LogCategory.DELETION_AUDIT, 90, 365, 730, 1095,
        "3 years: proves GDPR compliance without containing PII"
    ),
]

for p in POLICIES:
    window = f"{p.total_days} days" if p.total_days else "indefinite"
    print(f"{p.category.value:20s}  total={window:12s}  reason={p.justification[:50]}")
Classifying Your Logs Before Setting Policy

Not all LLM logs are equivalent. Before setting a retention window, identify which log category each record belongs to, because different categories have different risk profiles and regulatory requirements:
Operational logs
— structured records of latency, token counts, model name, cost, and finish_reason. No conversational content. These are safe to retain for 30–90 days and aggregate into dashboards indefinitely.
Security and audit logs
— records of who accessed the system, what API keys were used, and when deletions or exports occurred. Required by SOC 2 and ISO 27001 for 12 months minimum.
Content logs

— even redacted snippets of prompt and response text. These carry re-identification risk because an adversary with additional data can sometimes reconstruct the user's identity from behavioral patterns in the text. Keep for ≤30 days and only with a clear debugging justification.
Aggregate metrics
— daily counts, P95 latency, error rates, cost summaries. These contain no personal data and can be retained indefinitely for capacity planning and trend analysis.
Implementing Expiry with S3 Lifecycle Policies
If you write logs to S3 (or GCS/Azure Blob), lifecycle policies are the simplest way to enforce retention. They run automatically without application code.
Implementing Expiry in a Relational Database
For teams storing logs in PostgreSQL, table partitioning by date makes expiry a single
DROP TABLE
on an old partition — much faster than
DELETE WHERE created_at < ...
which locks the table and generates massive write amplification.

-- Create partitioned table (PostgreSQL 10+)
CREATE TABLE llm_operational_logs (
    id          UUID DEFAULT gen_random_uuid() PRIMARY KEY,
    logged_at   TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    model       TEXT NOT NULL,
    latency_ms  INT,
    prompt_tokens INT,
    completion_tokens INT,
    cost_usd    NUMERIC(10,6),
    finish_reason TEXT,
    session_token TEXT  -- pseudonymous, never real user_id
) PARTITION BY RANGE (logged_at);

-- Create monthly partitions
CREATE TABLE llm_logs_2026_06
    PARTITION OF llm_operational_logs
    FOR VALUES FROM ('2026-06-01') TO ('2026-07-01');

CREATE TABLE llm_logs_2026_07
    PARTITION OF llm_operational_logs
    FOR VALUES FROM ('2026-07-01') TO ('2026-08-01');

-- Dropping a partition is instant — no row-level lock, no WAL amplification
DROP TABLE llm_logs_2026_04;  -- delete all April 2026 logs in milliseconds

-- Automate with a scheduled job (e.g. pg_cron):
-- SELECT cron.schedule('drop-old-llm-logs', '0 2 1 * *',
--   $$DROP TABLE IF EXISTS llm_logs_<year>_<month_minus_3>$$);
Application-Level Retention Enforcement
When writing logs from Python, embed expiry metadata at write time. For Redis streams, use the
MAXLEN
trimming option or set explicit TTLs. For structured log systems, include a
delete_after
timestamp that your log pipeline can act on.

import logging
import json
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from enum import Enum

class LogCategory(Enum):
    OPERATIONAL = "operational"  # 90-day retention
    CONTENT = "content"          # 30-day retention
    AUDIT = "audit"              # 2-year retention
    AGGREGATE = "aggregate"      # indefinite


RETENTION_DAYS: dict[LogCategory, int | None] = {
    LogCategory.OPERATIONAL: 90,
    LogCategory.CONTENT: 30,
    LogCategory.AUDIT: 730,
    LogCategory.AGGREGATE: None,  # indefinite
}


def retention_aware_log(category: LogCategory, data: dict) -> dict:
    """
    Augment a log record with expiry metadata.
    The 'delete_after' field is consumed by the log pipeline or storage layer.
    """
    days = RETENTION_DAYS[category]
    delete_after = (
        (datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(days=days)).isoformat()
        if days is not None
        else None
    )
    return {
        **data,
        "log_category": category.value,
        "delete_after": delete_after,
        "logged_at": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
    }


logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


def log_llm_call(model: str, latency_ms: float, tokens: int) -> None:
    entry = retention_aware_log(
        LogCategory.OPERATIONAL,
        {"model": model, "latency_ms": latency_ms, "tokens": tokens},
    )
    logger.info(json.dumps(entry))


# Example log entry:
# {
#   "model": "gpt-4o",
#   "latency_ms": 1234.5,
#   "tokens": 512,
#   "log_category": "operational",
#   "delete_after": "2026-09-28T06:10:00+00:00",
#   "logged_at": "2026-06-28T06:10:00+00:00"
# }
Handling Right-to-Erasure Requests

Under GDPR Article 17, users can request that their data be deleted. For LLM logs this means deleting all records linked to a user's session tokens. The critical design constraint is that you must be able to perform this deletion
without
storing the mapping from session token to real user identity in the same store as the logs — otherwise the log store itself contains PII.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, timezone

# Vault: user_id → set of session_tokens
# Stored in a separate high-security database, NOT in the log store
USER_SESSION_VAULT: dict[str, set[str]] = {}  # use a DB in production


def register_session(user_id: str, session_token: str) -> None:
    """Call when a new session starts. Links user to their session tokens."""
    if user_id not in USER_SESSION_VAULT:
        USER_SESSION_VAULT[user_id] = set()
    USER_SESSION_VAULT[user_id].add(session_token)


def get_session_tokens(user_id: str) -> set[str]:
    """Retrieve all session tokens for a user to perform deletion."""
    return USER_SESSION_VAULT.get(user_id, set())


async def handle_erasure_request(
    user_id: str,
    log_db,  # async DB connection
) -> dict:
    """
    Delete all log records associated with a user's session tokens.
    Called when processing a GDPR right-to-erasure request.
    """
    session_tokens = get_session_tokens(user_id)
    if not session_tokens:
        return {"deleted_records": 0, "note": "No session data found"}

    # Delete from operational logs — partition-aware for performance
    deleted = await log_db.execute(
        "DELETE FROM llm_operational_logs WHERE session_token = ANY($1)",
        list(session_tokens),
    )

    # Delete from content logs
    deleted_content = await log_db.execute(
        "DELETE FROM llm_content_logs WHERE session_token = ANY($1)",
        list(session_tokens),
    )

    # Remove from vault — user no longer has any data
    USER_SESSION_VAULT.pop(user_id, None)

    # Log the erasure event (this log itself has no PII)
    erasure_record = {
        "event": "erasure_completed",
        "user_hash": hash_pii(user_id, "user_id"),  # hash, not raw ID
        "session_count": len(session_tokens),
        "records_deleted": deleted.rowcount + deleted_content.rowcount,
        "completed_at": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
    }
    # Store erasure_record in audit log (2-year retention)
    return erasure_record
Aggregate Before Deleting

The value of operational logs is in the patterns they reveal, not in the individual records. Before a batch of logs is deleted, run a daily aggregation job that computes summary statistics into a permanent, PII-free metrics table. This gives you indefinite historical visibility into model performance without retaining raw logs.

-- Run daily before deleting logs older than retention window
INSERT INTO llm_daily_metrics (
    date,
    model,
    request_count,
    error_count,
    p50_latency_ms,
    p95_latency_ms,
    p99_latency_ms,
    total_prompt_tokens,
    total_completion_tokens,
    total_cost_usd,
    refusal_count
)
SELECT
    DATE(logged_at)                           AS date,
    model,
    COUNT(*)                                  AS request_count,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE finish_reason != 'stop') AS error_count,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.50) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY latency_ms) AS p50_latency_ms,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.95) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY latency_ms) AS p95_latency_ms,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.99) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY latency_ms) AS p99_latency_ms,
    SUM(prompt_tokens)                        AS total_prompt_tokens,
    SUM(completion_tokens)                    AS total_completion_tokens,
    SUM(cost_usd)                             AS total_cost_usd,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE is_refusal = TRUE) AS refusal_count
FROM llm_operational_logs
WHERE logged_at >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day'
  AND logged_at  < CURRENT_DATE
GROUP BY DATE(logged_at), model
ON CONFLICT (date, model) DO UPDATE
    SET request_count = EXCLUDED.request_count,
        p95_latency_ms = EXCLUDED.p95_latency_ms;
-- After this job, the raw partition can be dropped safely
Key principle:

Set retention windows before your first log is written, not after. Retroactively deleting logs from a live system is error-prone and expensive. The right time to implement partition-based expiry, S3 lifecycle rules, and aggregation jobs is during the initial logging infrastructure setup — when it takes hours rather than the days it takes to retrofit.
Key Takeaways
Retention policy = structured deletion:
define how long each log category lives before automatic, permanent removal.
Classify logs first:
operational metrics, security audit, conversation content, and aggregates each require different windows because they have different risk profiles and regulatory requirements.
Even redacted content logs
carry re-identification risk through behavioral fingerprinting — treat them with the same caution as raw PII and keep for ≤30 days.
Make expiry structural:
S3 lifecycle rules, PostgreSQL partition drops, and Redis TTLs enforce deletion automatically without application code that can be accidentally disabled.
Aggregate before deleting:
compute daily P95 latency, error rates, and token counts into a PII-free metrics table before dropping the raw partition. Retain aggregates indefinitely.
Right-to-erasure path:
store the user→session_token mapping in a separate high-security vault, not the log store. Erasure then becomes: lookup tokens, delete records, remove vault entry, log the erasure event.
Security audit logs are the exception:
SOC 2 and ISO 27001 require 12–24 months minimum for access logs. Shorten content logs; lengthen audit logs.
Build retention in at day 0,
not after your first regulator inquiry. Retrofitting partition-based expiry and lifecycle rules into a running system is an order of magnitude more expensive than designing for it from the start.
